" Field MATNR: Format = CHAR, Length = 18 (old DDIC)
" After DDIC change: MATNR in structure = CHAR 40

" Fix: Regenerate screen in SE51
" SE51 → Screen 0200 → Activate (F3 or menu)
" Screen now reads DDIC with length 40 — mismatch resolved

" Also check ABAP variable:
" Wrong: DATA: lv_matnr TYPE c LENGTH 18.
" Correct: DATA: lv_matnr TYPE matnr. " Use DDIC type reference
" Using DDIC type means auto-sync when DDIC changes

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you reproduce a production Module Pool issue in development?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Golden rule: kabhi bhi production mein directly debug mat karo. Development mein reproduce karo. Reproduction steps: ST22 se exact: program name, include, line number, error variables values note karo. Development system mein same T-code run karo same data ke saath (same plant, same material, same batch as production). Agar data development mein nahi hai: data banao ya production se copy karo (data privacy rules follow karke). Same scenario: same user type, same input sequence. Agar reproduce nahi ho raha: additional logging add karo development mein — MESSAGE W ya write to Z log table at each step. BREAK-USER breakpoint lagao development mein (specific user ke liye).
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I never debug directly in production. I start by reading ST22 dump details — exact program, line, variable values, and error category. Then in development I set a breakpoint at or before the reported line and replicate the exact input scenario using the same data as production. If production data is needed, I create equivalent test data in development following data privacy guidelines. For intermittent issues I add temporary logging via WRITE TO custom log table at key PAI points in development, reproduce the scenario, and read the log to narrow down the exact condition causing the failure.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Issue: Screen dump only happens when 48 or more rows in Table Control. Could not reproduce with 10 rows. Investigated: tc_items-lines variable — with 48 rows an internal table index exceeded bounds in PAI LOOP. Added temporary MESSAGE W in LOOP to show sy-tabix. Reproduced with 48 rows in development. Found: MODIFY lt_items FROM ls_item INDEX sy-tabix — sy-tabix exceeded table bounds. Fix: added bounds check.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Production mein BREAK-USER breakpoint lagana — aur phir bhool jaana remove karna. Next user jo woh program run karta hai debugger mein atak jaata hai. NEVER leave breakpoints in production. Always use BREAK-USER with your own user ID only in development.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Safe debugging in development only:
" 1. Set BREAK-USER breakpoint for your user
BREAK-USER sy-uname.  " Only stops for your user, safe in dev

" 2. Or conditional breakpoint:
IF sy-tabix = 48.  " Suspect row count
  BREAK-USER sy-uname.  " Stop here for investigation
ENDIF.

" 3. Temporary logging (dev only, remove before transport)
DATA: lv_log_msg TYPE string.
lv_log_msg = |PAI row { sy-tabix }: mat={ ls_item-matnr }|.
MESSAGE lv_log_msg TYPE 'W'.  " Remove before transport!

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use SM13 for Module Pool data save issues?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SM13 = Update Monitor. SAP mein UPDATE TASK mechanism hai — database writes ko background task mein defer kiya ja sakta hai performance ke liye. Kuch Module Pool programs CALL FUNCTION ... IN UPDATE TASK use karte hain. Agar update task fail kare: user ko message dikh sakti hai 'Update terminated', ya silently fail. SM13 mein: failed update tasks list hoti hain — program name, user, timestamp, error message. Failed updates ko SM13 se restart bhi kar sakte hain (short-term workaround). Root cause: koi exception update task mein handle nahi hua, ya DB error. Fix karo root cause aur re-run.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SM13 shows the update task monitor where failed asynchronous database updates appear. When a Module Pool program uses CALL FUNCTION IN UPDATE TASK for deferred writes, if the update task fails the user may see an error notification or the save appears to succeed but data is missing. In SM13 I filter by program name and user, read the error details of the failed update entry, and can restart the update as a short-term fix. The root cause — usually an unhandled exception or database error in the update function module — is then fixed in development.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Support ticket: user bol raha tha 'Save successful message aaya but material document nahi bana'. SM13 mein: ZGISS_UPDATE function module failed — error: 'Number range not defined for document type'. Number range configuration missing tha in production. SPRO se number range configure kiya, SM13 se failed update restart kiya — document created.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: SM13 check karna bhool jaana jab user kahe 'data save nahi hua'. Direct code mein jaana without checking SM13. SM13 often shows the root cause directly — configuration, auth, DB error — saving hours of code investigation.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SM13 investigation steps:
" 1. SM13 → Enter user name → Select date range
" 2. Look for 'Error' status entries
" 3. Select entry → Display → Read error message
" 4. Common SM13 errors for Module Pool:
"    - Number range not defined
"    - Authorization failure in update task
"    - Database table locked
"    - DB insert failure (duplicate key)

" Module Pool with UPDATE TASK (pattern that SM13 monitors):
CALL FUNCTION 'ZGISS_DO_SAVE'
  IN UPDATE TASK             " Deferred to update work process
  EXPORTING is_header = ls_header
             it_items  = lt_items.
COMMIT WORK.                 " Triggers the update task
" SM13 will show this function if it fails

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between P1 and P2 incident handling for Module Pool?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: P1 (Priority 1) = Critical business impact. Sab users affected hain, business process completely blocked. Example: ZGOODSISS transaction sabke liye dump de raha hai, koi goods issue post nahi kar sakta, warehouse operations stopped. Action: immediate. Emergency transport process use karo (usually requires manager approval aur change management override). P2 (Priority 2) = High impact but workaround possible. Kuch users affected, ya partial functionality. Example: Delete Row button kaam nahi kar raha Table Control mein — users manually row blank kar sakte hain. Action: same day fix, normal transport. P1 mein: sabse senior developer ya consultant immediately assign, root cause analysis parallel with fix, manager communication every 30 min.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: P1 incidents mean complete business process stoppage — all users of the transaction are blocked with no workaround. For module pool P1, I immediately replicate the issue in development, identify and fix the root cause, and follow the emergency transport process which typically requires change advisory board approval bypass with post-facto documentation. Communication every 30 minutes to management. P2 means partial impact with a workaround available — same-day fix using standard transport, thorough testing before transport.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real P1: module pool T-code giving dump for ALL users after a Basis team transport accidentally overwrote a screen. Immediate action: identified wrong screen was transported (older version), raised emergency retransport of correct screen version. Fix deployed in 45 minutes. Root cause: missing transport dependency check by Basis.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: P1 incident pe thorough testing karne mein time lagaana — agar business is blocked, 80% confident fix with fast deployment better hai. Perfect solution baad mein refine kar sakte hain. Speed matters in P1.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" P1 Incident response checklist:
" [ ] 1. Verify incident scope (all users? specific users?)
" [ ] 2. ST22 — identify dump, read error
" [ ] 3. Provide workaround (use alternate T-code?)
" [ ] 4. Replicate in development
" [ ] 5. Fix and unit test
" [ ] 6. Emergency CAB approval for transport
" [ ] 7. Transport to production
" [ ] 8. Verify fix with 2-3 test users before announcing
" [ ] 9. Monitor SM50/ST22 for 30 min post-fix
" [ ] 10. Root cause analysis document (RCA)

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle a Module Pool screen that shows stale data after navigation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Stale data issue: user Screen 100 pe data enter kiya, Screen 200 pe gaya, wapas Screen 100 pe aaya — Screen 100 pe pehle wala data nahi, koi aur data hai ya blank hai. Cause: PBO mein CLEAR ya re-initialization missing. Global variable jo Screen 100 pe display hoti hai — woh Screen 200 ke PAI ne change kar di. Fix: PBO mein Screen 100 ke pehle relevant global variables clear karo ya re-initialize karo fresh data se. Alternative: Screen 100 pe aate waqt DB se fresh read karo taaki hamesha latest data dikhiye. SPA/GPA se key preserve karo toh DB se fresh read karo PBO mein.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Stale data on screen navigation occurs when global variables shared between screens are modified by one screen's PAI but not refreshed in another screen's PBO. The fix is to add proper initialization logic in the PBO of the affected screen — either clearing variables before populating them, or re-reading from the database using the preserved key. I use GET PARAMETER ID to retain the record key across navigation, then in PBO do a fresh SELECT to populate the screen with current database data.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Issue: vendor module pool, screen 100 showing vendor number entered on previous run. PBO not clearing ls_header before displaying. Fix: in PBO 0100, added CLEAR ls_header before any initialization. If returning from screen 200 with saved data, read from Z custom table using header key. Fresh data every time screen 100 displays.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: PBO mein sirf SET PF-STATUS aur SET TITLEBAR — koi data initialization nahi. Sochna ki data automatically correct rahega. Hamesha PBO mein clear aur re-initialize karo display data.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Fix for stale data — PBO with proper initialization
MODULE pbo_0100 OUTPUT.
  SET PF-STATUS 'ZSTATUS'.
  SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.
  " Clear before populate — no stale data
  CLEAR ls_header.
  " If we have a saved key, read fresh from DB
  IF gv_saved_key IS NOT INITIAL.
    SELECT SINGLE * FROM zgiss_header
      WHERE zdoc = gv_saved_key
      INTO @ls_header.
  ELSE.
    " New entry — set defaults
    ls_header-werks = sy-werks.
    ls_header-erdat = sy-datum.
  ENDIF.
ENDMODULE.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you monitor a Module Pool transaction post go-live?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Go-live ke baad monitoring approach: Pehle 2 weeks — active monitoring. ST22 daily check karo — koi naya dump toh nahi. SM13 daily check karo — failed updates toh nahi. Custom log table ZGISS_LOG se daily report nikaalo — kitne transactions, success rate, error patterns. SM20 (Security Audit Log) — unauthorized access attempts. SLG1 (Application Log) — agar application log banaya hai module pool mein. User feedback channel rakhho — dedicated email ya SAP message. Agar error rate > 2% hai toh investigate. Weekly: performance trend — SM50 average response times. Monthly: usage trend — ZGISS_LOG se user adoption metrics.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Post go-live monitoring includes daily checks of ST22 for new dumps related to the module pool program, SM13 for failed update tasks, and the custom audit log table for transaction success rates and error patterns. I set up a simple daily report using a background job that reads the log table and emails error summaries. For the first two weeks I also hold daily 15-minute check-in calls with key users to catch usability issues early. Any error rate above 2% triggers immediate root cause investigation.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Post go-live week 1: ZGISS_LOG se pata chala 8% transactions failing — all with same error: batch not found. Investigation: users entering batch in lowercase, validation doing exact match (case-sensitive). Fix: TRANSLATE ls_item-charg TO UPPER CASE before validation. Error rate dropped to 0.3%. Log table ne iss pattern ko identify karne mein help kiya — impossible to catch in testing.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Go-live ke baad monitoring band kar dena — 'transaction live hai, kaam ho gaya'. Production mein real user behavior testing mein nahi aata. First 4 weeks monitoring mandatory hai naye module pool ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Background job for daily monitoring — simple SELECT
REPORT zgiss_daily_monitor.
DATA: lv_date   TYPE sy-datum,
      lv_total  TYPE i,
      lv_errors TYPE i.
lv_date = sy-datum - 1.  " Yesterday
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM zgiss_log
  WHERE zdate = @lv_date INTO @lv_total.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM zgiss_log
  WHERE zdate = @lv_date AND zstatus = 'E'
  INTO @lv_errors.
IF lv_total > 0.
  DATA(lv_error_pct) = ( lv_errors * 100 ) / lv_total.
  IF lv_error_pct > 2.  " Alert threshold
    " Send email to support team
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.
